name: Acne Vulgaris
description: A common skin condition characterized by clogged pores, inflammation, and the formation of pimples, blackheads, and whiteheads, typically appearing on the face, chest, and back.
when_to_see_dermatologist: Persistent acne despite 2-3 months of OTC treatment
Acne affecting quality of life or self-esteem
Signs of infection (increased pain, warmth, fever)
Hormonal patterns suggesting underlying endocrine issue
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Acne Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Acne Treatment Updates
Cochrane Review: Topical Treatments for Acne

name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
description: A chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry, itchy, and inflamed skin, often associated with allergies and asthma, commonly affecting flexural areas like elbows and knees.
symptoms: Small raised bumps that may leak fluid
Commonly affects cheeks, elbows, knees, wrists, ankles
when_to_see_dermatologist: Signs of infection (yellow crusting, pain, fever)
Failure to respond to OTC treatments after 2-4 weeks
Significant impact on sleep or quality of life
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Atopic Dermatitis Guidelines
Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology - Eczema Management
Cochrane Review: Emollients for Eczema

name: Rosacea
description: A chronic inflammatory skin condition primarily affecting the central face, characterized by flushing, persistent redness, visible blood vessels, and sometimes acne-like bumps and eye irritation.
symptoms: Persistent redness on cheeks, nose, chin, forehead
evidence_based_treatments: Trigger avoidance (heat, alcohol, spicy foods, stress)
when_to_see_dermatologist: Acne-like breakouts that don't respond to acne treatments
Uncertain diagnosis or need for prescription treatment
related_articles: National Rosacea Society Expert Committee Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Rosacea Updates
Cochrane Review: Treatments for Rosacea

name: Psoriasis
description: A chronic autoimmune condition that causes rapid buildup of skin cells, leading to thick, scaly patches that are often itchy, painful, and inflamed, commonly affecting elbows, knees, scalp, and lower back.
symptoms: Red patches covered with thick, silvery scales
Dry, cracked skin that may bleed
Patches that vary from few spots to major eruptions
when_to_see_dermatologist: Failure to respond to OTC treatments
Need for prescription or systemic treatment
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Psoriasis Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Psoriasis Treatment
Cochrane Review: Treatments for Psoriasis

name: Contact Dermatitis
description: A rash caused by skin contact with irritants or allergens, resulting in red, itchy, inflamed skin that may blister, peel, or feel burning, divided into irritant and allergic types.
symptoms: Bumps and blisters that may ooze
when_to_see_dermatologist: Signs of infection (pus, increasing pain, fever)
Persistent rash despite avoidance and OTC treatment
Suspected allergic contact dermatitis needing patch testing
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Contact Dermatitis Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Contact Dermatitis Updates
Cochrane Review: Topical Steroids for Contact Dermatitis

name: Melasma
description: A common hyperpigmentation disorder characterized by brown to gray-brown patches, typically appearing on the face (cheeks, forehead, nose, upper lip), often triggered by hormonal changes and sun exposure.
symptoms: Brown or gray-brown patches on face
Patches commonly on cheeks, bridge of nose, forehead, chin, upper lip
Often worsens during pregnancy or with hormonal contraceptives
May fade in winter, worsen in summer
evidence_based_treatments: Discontinuation of triggering hormones when possible
when_to_see_dermatologist: Pigmentation that doesn't fade with months of sun protection
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Hyperpigmentation Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Melasma Treatment
Cochrane Review: Treatments for Melasma

name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
description: A common inflammatory skin condition that causes scaly patches, red skin, and stubborn dandruff, primarily affecting oily areas like scalp, face (eyebrows, nose, ears), and chest.
symptoms: Stubborn dandruff on scalp, eyebrows, beard, or mustache
Commonly affects scalp, face (nasolabial folds, eyebrows, ears), chest
May worsen with stress, cold weather, or hormonal changes
evidence_based_treatments: Medicated shampoos (ketoconazole, selenium sulfide, zinc pyrithione)
when_to_see_dermatologist: Failure to respond to OTC medicated shampoos after 4-6 weeks
Uncertain diagnosis or overlapping with psoriasis/eczema
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Seborrheic Dermatitis Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Seborrheic Dermatitis Updates
Cochrane Review: Antifungals for Seborrheic Dermatitis

name: Actinic Keratosis
description: A precancerous skin condition caused by years of sun exposure, characterized by rough, scaly patches on sun-exposed areas that can potentially develop into squamous cell carcinoma if left untreated.
symptoms: Color varies from pink to red to brown
May feel sore or tender when touched
Commonly on face, lips, ears, forearms, scalp, neck
More common in fair-skinned individuals with sun history
when_to_see_dermatologist: Any new or changing skin lesion
Fair skin with light eyes and hair
Lesions that bleed, grow, or change appearance
Need for professional diagnosis and treatment
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Actinic Keratosis Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Pre-Cancerous Lesions
Cochrane Review: Treatments for Actinic Keratosis

name: Hyperhidrosis
description: A medical condition characterized by excessive sweating beyond what's needed for temperature regulation, most commonly affecting palms, soles, underarms, and face, often causing significant emotional and social impact.
symptoms: Visible sweating that soaks through clothing
Skin maceration or breakdown from constant moisture
Unpleasant odor from bacterial breakdown of sweat
Sweating that interferes with daily activities
when_to_see_dermatologist: Excessive sweating interfering with daily life
Failure to respond to clinical-strength antiperspirants
Skin irritation or infection from constant moisture
Need for prescription treatments or procedures
Uncertain diagnosis or rule out medical causes
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Hyperhidrosis Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Hyperhidrosis Treatment
Cochrane Review: Treatments for Hyperhidrosis

name: Alopecia Areata
description: An autoimmune condition where the immune system attacks hair follicles, leading to sudden hair loss in small, round patches on the scalp, face, or other body areas, which may progress to larger areas or complete hair loss.
symptoms: Sudden loss of hair in round or oval patches
Smooth, bald patches without inflammation or scarring
Patches typically a few centimeters in diameter
May affect scalp, beard, eyebrows, eyelashes, or body hair
Sometimes preceded by tingling or itching in area
Hair may regrow then fall out again
Nail changes (pitting, ridges, roughness) in some cases
Can progress to total scalp hair loss (alopecia totalis) or total body hair loss (alopecia universalis)
evidence_based_treatments: Wigs or hairpieces for cosmetic coverage
when_to_see_dermatologist: Hair loss in eyebrows, eyelashes, or beard
Need for prescription or injection treatments
Uncertain diagnosis or rule out other causes
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Alopecia Areata Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Alopecia Treatment
Cochrane Review: Treatments for Alopecia Areata

name: Vitiligo
description: An autoimmune condition that causes loss of skin color in patches when melanocytes (cells that produce pigment) are destroyed or stop functioning, resulting in white patches that can appear anywhere on the body.
symptoms: White or light-colored patches on skin
Commonly affects hands, feet, arms, face, lips
Patches may start small and grow over time
Premature whitening or graying of hair on scalp, eyelashes, eyebrows, beard
Loss of color in mucous membranes (mouth, nose)
Patches often symmetrical but can be asymmetrical
More noticeable in darker skin tones
when_to_see_dermatologist: Whitening of hair in patches or scalp
Need for prescription treatments or phototherapy
Uncertain diagnosis or rule out other causes
Cosmetic concerns impacting quality of life
Family history or associated autoimmune conditions
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Vitiligo Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Vitiligo Treatment
Cochrane Review: Treatments for Vitiligo

name: Hives (Urticaria)
description: A skin reaction that causes raised, itchy welts (wheals) that can vary in size and shape, appearing suddenly and often resolving within hours to days, though chronic cases can persist for months or years.
symptoms: Welts that vary in size from small to large
Welts that may join together to form larger areas
Swelling (angioedema) around eyes, lips, hands, feet, or throat
Welts that change shape, move, disappear, and reappear
when_to_see_dermatologist: Difficulty breathing or swallowing (signs of anaphylaxis)
Swelling of lips, tongue, or throat
Uncertain diagnosis or rule out underlying causes
History of severe reactions requiring epinephrine
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Urticaria Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Hives Treatment
Cochrane Review: Antihistamines for Urticaria

name: Keratosis Pilaris
description: A common, harmless skin condition that causes dry, rough patches and tiny bumps, usually on the upper arms, thighs, cheeks, or buttocks, often described as 'chicken skin' due to the buildup of keratin blocking hair follicles.
symptoms: Tiny papules that feel like sandpaper
Typically affects upper arms, thighs, buttocks, cheeks
May have slight redness or inflammation around bumps
Skin feels dry and rough to touch
Often worse in winter or low humidity
Usually begins in childhood and may improve with age
More common in people with dry skin or eczema
evidence_based_treatments: Gentle exfoliation (lactic acid, glycolic acid, urea)
Humidifiers to add moisture to air
Avoiding harsh soaps and hot water
Patience - condition often improves with age
when_to_see_dermatologist: Uncertain diagnosis or rule out other conditions
Accompanying symptoms like weight loss or fatigue
Desire for professional evaluation and guidance
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Keratosis Pilaris Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - KP Treatment Updates
Cochrane Review: Emollients and Exfoliants for Keratosis Pilaris

name: Dry Skin (Xerosis)
description: A common condition where skin lacks sufficient moisture in its outer layer, leading to roughness, tightness, flaking, and sometimes itching or cracking, particularly prevalent in older adults and during winter months.
symptoms: Feeling of tightness, especially after washing
Fine lines or cracks that may appear
Ashy or gray appearance in darker skin tones
More pronounced on arms, legs, hands, feet
Worsens in cold, dry weather or low humidity
May feel better after applying moisturizer
evidence_based_treatments: Humidifiers to add moisture to air
Avoid harsh soaps, alcohols, and fragrances
Occlusive agents (petrolatum, mineral oil) for severe cases
when_to_see_dermatologist: Severe or persistent dryness despite moisturizing
Signs of infection (cracks that bleed, ooze, or show red streaks)
Underlying conditions like diabetes, thyroid disorders, or kidney disease
Need for prescription treatments or underlying cause evaluation
Significant impact on quality of life or comfort
Uncertain diagnosis or rule out ichthyosis or other disorders
Associated symptoms like weight loss, fatigue, or fever
related_articles: American Academy of Dermatology Dry Skin Guidelines
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology - Xerosis Treatment
Cochrane Review: Emollients for Dry Skin
